g.data-narration: Welcome to Scalable Computing Over the Internet. Let's explore how modern systems handle growing workloads efficiently.
Scalable computing is a system's ability to handle growing workloads by enlarging resources to accommodate growth efficiently.
g.text: The ability of a computing system to handle growing workloads by being
enlarged to accommodate growth. Essential for modern internet-based
applications serving millions of users with variable demand patterns.

g.data-narration: Computing evolved from standalone machines to cloud computing: client-server to cluster, grid, and finally cloud paradigms.
NIST defines five essential characteristics: elasticity, on-demand self-service, broad network access, resource pooling, and measured service.
Vertical scaling adds more resources (CPU, RAM, storage) to a single server. It's simpler but has physical limits and creates a single point of failure.
Horizontal scaling adds more servers to distribute the load. It offers virtually unlimited scaling with fault tolerance but requires distributed architecture.
This comparison shows vertical scaling with one powerful server versus horizontal scaling with multiple commodity servers working together.
Virtualization enables multiple virtual machines on one physical server, maximizing resource utilization through hypervisors.
Load balancers distribute network traffic across multiple servers, ensuring no single server becomes overwhelmed and improving reliability.
Three main models: Cluster (tightly connected local computers), Grid (geographically distributed resources), and Cloud (internet-based on-demand services).

svg.g:
Computing Models Comparison
Cluster Computing Local, tightly coupled systems
Grid Computing Distributed, heterogeneous, shared
Cloud Computing On-demand, provider-managed
Use cases: HPC clusters, Research grids, Web apps respectively </g|
Cloud Service Models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS)
IaaS Infrastructure as a Service VM, Storage, Networks
PaaS Platform as a Service Runtime, DB, Dev Tools
SaaS Software as a Service Gmail, Office 365, Netflix
Customer Manages
Provider Manages
Challenges in Scalable Internet Computing
Network Latency Delay in distributed comm.
Data Consistency Hard across distributed sys.
Security Concerns Vulnerable to threats
Cost Management Unexpected scaling costs
Management Complex distributed ops
Real-World Examples
Google Search Billions of queries daily
AWS Millions of customers
Netflix Demand-based scaling
Social Media Massive variable traffic
Future Trends
Edge Computing Process closer to source
Serverless No server management
AI Autoscaling ML-driven scaling
Quantum Future computing
Exam Tips & Key Takeaways
1. Know the difference between vertical and horizontal scaling 2. Memorize NIST's 5 characteristics (Elasticity, On-demand, Broad access, Pooling, Measured) 3. Compare cluster, grid, and cloud computing models
